title: Robot Livery
description: Brief description of the project and your role.

caseStudy.overview: Agility Robotics produces experimental video content featuring Digit in R&D and innovation contexts — but customers were sometimes confused about whether they were watching production-ready behavior or early-stage experimentation. To solve this, I designed a custom livery: a physical wrap applied to Digit's chest shells that creates an unmistakable visual distinction between the production robot and its experimental counterpart.
caseStudy.team: Graphic Designer (me), Marketing Team, Sales Team, Innovation Team, Local Wrap Shop
objectives: Visually distinguish Digit when operating in experimental or R&D contexts
Eliminate customer confusion between production-ready and experimental robot behavior
Create a design that reads clearly on camera for use in video content
Maintain Agility's brand identity while signaling a clearly different mode or context
caseStudy.challenge: The core design challenge was communicating 'experimental / future state' in a way that was visually obvious without abandoning Agility's brand. The livery had to work on camera first and foremost — this was conceived as a solution for video content — while also physically surviving the rigors of being applied to a working robot. The wrap goes on existing chest shells that snap onto the robot's torso, so the design had to account for the robot's form factor and range of movement.
caseStudy.approach: I researched how other companies use visual language to signal R&D or prototype status versus production. My design process involved many iterations across the marketing, sales, and innovation teams before landing on a final direction. For the physical production, I managed the relationship with a local wrap shop — overseeing application and ensuring color accuracy matched the design intent.

userExperience.description: The primary 'audience' for this livery is the camera — viewers watching Agility's video content. The design needed to be immediately readable as 'different' without requiring explanation, while still looking intentional and on-brand rather than like a mistake or placeholder.
insights: The livery primarily needed to work on camera, not just in person — video readability drove design decisions
Visual distinction had to be immediate and self-explanatory to avoid adding friction for viewers
Color accuracy between digital design and physical wrap output was critical to maintaining brand fidelity

caseStudy.solution: The final livery design uses a distinct visual approach — different enough from Digit's standard appearance to clearly signal 'experimental,' but cohesive enough to still feel like an intentional Agility product. Applied as a physical wrap to the robot's chest shells by a local wrap shop, the livery is purpose-built for video content and has appeared across Agility's experimental and innovation-focused productions.

images.caption: Livery design applied to Digit's chest shells
On-camera appearance in experimental video content

results: Created a clear, camera-ready visual distinction between production and experimental versions of Digit
Design approved after multiple iterations across marketing, sales, and innovation stakeholders
Wrap successfully applied by local vendor with accurate color reproduction
Livery has been used in Agility's experimental and R&D video content
